As a contract recruiter, give me a scope of work over a job description every time! 
 
I filled a role with a new client last week – best of all, they called me!

I pride myself on sourcing top quality candidates, usually from my existing network, in minimal time. On this occasion the role was slightly outside of my core market, so I had to qualify candidates not already in my network, but I was still able to find the perfect candidate in less than 24 hours.  

I was only able to do this because the hiring manager shared the scope of work for the project with me, rather than a job description. 
This allowed me to:
-         Refine my search to find profiles with very specific experience.
-         Clearly articulate what the project would be and what would be required from the contractor.
-         Qualify candidates on when they had delivered similar projects, rather than what key skills they have. 
-         Get candidates excited about the opportunity.

I’d love to hear the thoughts of other contract recruiters, experienced contractors or hiring managers. 

DISCLAIMER - Yes the image in this post has been made with AI but the content is all me!  

Ryan Collett Stephen Davis Adam Watson Zak Wells Gavin Lewis John Cullen

🚨 POV: $2T Shift No One Is Calling Out Clearly.

This is not digital transformation. This is not AI-led growth.
This is CONTROL moving back to enterprises.

What’s actually happening beneath the noise: Across AI, GCC expansion, and vendor compression, we’re seeing a potential:

👉 $1.5T–$2T redistribution of control

Not new value.
Reassigned value.

Breakdown:

• ~$600B → AI-driven productivity (30–40% effort collapse)
• ~$500B → GCC insourcing (3–4x hiring vs services in key markets)
• ~$400B → Platform consolidation (fewer vendors, deeper lock-in)
• ~$300B → Services compression (pricing + scope reset)

🧠 Structural shift (most are missing this):
- CONTROL → Enterprises (AI, data, IP, decisioning)
- PLATFORM → Product ecosystems (owning workflows)
- EXECUTION → Services (integration, orchestration, AI layering)

👉Services are no longer “builders”, they are becoming system orchestrators.

⚠️ Hard signals already visible in live environments:
• Large deals fragmenting: $150M–$200M programs → $40M–$80M modular contracts

• Core engineering quietly moving in-house: 60–70% shift in strategic areas

• Pricing pressure compounding: 20–30% drop + 30% effort reduction from AI

👉 That’s not optimization. That’s a structural margin reset.

💬 Truth:

“Enterprises didn’t lose capability. They outsourced it. Now with AI they’re buying it back. Faster.”

🚀 What happens next (next 3–5 years):
• Enterprises will own AI, data, and core IP layers
• GCCs will become innovation engines—not cost centers
• Services firms will compete on:
→ Speed
→ Integration depth
→ AI orchestration capability

Not headcount.

⚡Take:

This isn’t a cycle. This is a once-in-a-decade control reset.

And it’s already showing up in:
* Deal pipelines
* Hiring patterns
* Vendor consolidation decisions

👇 Question (for leaders actually in it):

Are you already seeing:
→ AI reducing deal sizes?
→ More work pulled into GCCs?
→ Clients reclaiming core engineering?

Or is this still “future talk” in your world?

#AITransformation #GlobalCapabilityCenters #EnterpriseAI #ITServices #DigitalTransformation #FutureOfWork #TechStrategy #CXO #CIO #CTO #SVP #VP

The scariest tech metric right now isn't an AI benchmark.

It's 177.

Vaibhav Sisinty dropped this number on Raj Shamani's podcast — and I had to rewind it twice.

In the last 12 months, the top 5 IT firms in India — TCS, Infosys, Wipro, HCL, and Tech Mahindra — had a combined net headcount addition of 177 employees.

Not 1,77,000. Not 17,700. 177.

These same companies were hiring lakhs every single year not long ago. The engine hasn't slowed. It's been structurally dismantled.

---

A trillion dollars was wiped off global IT stocks — not from a recession — but because investors did one calculation: if AI agents handle finance, ops, marketing, and support, you don't need the headcount.

Salesforce Agentforce. ServiceNow AI. Already in enterprise contracts.

---

The Labour Arbitrage model is structurally finished. Revenue kept growing. Headcount collapsed. That decoupling is the whole story.

The era of the Safe Employee is over. The only asset that compounds in an AI-first economy is Leverage.

---

You don't have to found a company. But you have to become a Builder.

A Builder doesn't execute tasks — they build systems that do.
A Builder doesn't sell hours — they sell outcomes.

As Vaibhav said: "Becoming an employee was the most stable thing. Building something of your own — right now — is the most stable thing."

That's not inspiration. That's survival architecture.

---

Two camps are forming: those waiting for the hiring cycle to return, and those who've accepted it isn't.

Which camp are you building from?

↓ And the real question: Is this a structural collapse of the labour arbitrage model — or a cyclical dip dressed in AI clothing?

Drop your read. I'll engage with every serious take.

#IndianIT #FutureOfWork #AI #LaborArbitrage #AgenticAI #Builder #RajShamani #VaibhavSisinty #TCS #Infosys #Wipro #HCLTech #TechMahindra

I've talked a lot about how macro instability - particularly geopolitical tension and technological change - has been a tailwind for fractional and interim hiring, pushing companies toward optionality over commitment. That's the demand side. But there's an equally significant force reshaping the supply side: AI.

Fractional execs who've genuinely embedded AI into the way they operate are becoming meaningfully more productive. 

This shows up in two ways:

1. Delivery to clients

A fractional CFO who used to spend days building a board pack or financial model can now produce one in hours. A fractional COO can synthesise operational data across a client's tools and surface a diagnostic in the first week, not month. 

The ramp-up time that used to constrain interim engagements is compressing fast, and challenging the common push back: "how deep can they really go?".

2. Internal admin and ops

Most fractional execs are essentially solopreneurs, and the admin stack that comes with managing multiple clients - proposals, contracts, scheduling, invoicing, knowledge management - used to eat a serious chunk of productive time. AI has significantly alleviated this burden.

The net effect is that the fractional & interim operators leveraging AI effectively can serve more clients at a higher standard.

They bring the strategic judgement and context that no tool replaces, and they've offloaded the execution and administrative drag that used to cap what one person could take on.

With both demand rising and supply getting stronger, the fractional model is structurally improving.

Contract hiring in DevOps is no longer a backup plan.
It is becoming the first choice for delivery.

Over the past 6 to 12 months, hiring has shifted from building teams to solving problems. Budgets are tighter, timelines are shorter, and there is less room for slow hiring processes.

That is where contract comes in.

When the scope is clear, bringing in someone for a defined piece of work is often faster and more effective than trying to hire permanently. 

Whether it is a cloud migration, a platform rebuild, or an AI proof of concept, the focus is on outcomes rather than headcount.

It is a different way of thinking about hiring, but it is one that a lot of teams are leaning into.

A Product Manager walks into a sprint review and says, “We need speed, flexibility, and zero long-term commitments.” HR nods…and hires contractors.

That mindset took me back to 2015, when I was a Northeastern University intern at an insurance product company in the U.S. I walked into the IT team expecting a traditional setup—only to find nearly 25 people, most of them contractors. It felt less like a plug-and-play engine built for delivery.

Back then, contractors were the PM’s secret weapon—need a data pipeline? Call a contractor. Dashboard by Friday? Contractor. Production bug at 2 AM? Definitely a contractor.

But here’s the twist—cloud + AI just rewrote the playbook. With platforms like Google Cloud Platform and Amazon Web Services, teams can build reusable, automated systems instead of repeatedly hiring for the same tasks. Add agentic AI, and suddenly your “contractor” is an AI agent that doesn’t need onboarding or coffee breaks ☕.

AI comment: My AI agent asked for a promotion…turns out it already automated my job description.

So, do we end contract culture? Not fully—but we reduce dependency by building smarter, cloud-native, AI-driven teams.

In today’s evolving IT landscape, layoffs have created uncertainty—but they’ve also pushed organizations to rethink how they build and scale teams.

One of the smartest shifts I’m seeing is the increased adoption of staff augmentation and contract-based resources.

Here’s why this model is becoming a game-changer:

🔹 Cost Efficiency
Instead of long-term fixed costs, companies can align expenses directly with project needs. No overheads of full-time hiring, onboarding, or bench costs.

🔹 Faster Time-to-Market
Need a skilled resource quickly? Contract professionals can be onboarded in days, not months—helping teams accelerate delivery timelines.

🔹 Access to Specialized Skills
Whether it’s cloud migration, AI/ML, or legacy modernization, you can bring in niche expertise exactly when required.

🔹 Scalability & Flexibility
Scale your team up or down based on workload. This agility is critical in uncertain market conditions.

🔹 Reduced Risk
Short-term engagements allow organizations to evaluate talent and adapt strategies without long-term commitments.

In a time where efficiency, speed, and adaptability define success, staff augmentation is not just a cost-saving tactic—it’s a strategic advantage.
Companies that embrace this model will not only survive market fluctuations but also build more resilient and future-ready teams.

Paralegal Supply Is Pricing This Wrong

There are currently 47,000 open paralegal and legal assistant postings on LinkedIn in the United States. That number has barely moved since January. What has moved is the number of applicants per posting: up roughly 340 percent over the same period, according to LinkedIn's own hiring-trend data published in March. The ratio broke something in the usual equilibrium. Law firms are not hiring faster. Candidates are not finding work faster. The postings are becoming a kind of fiction. The underlying mechanism is not mysterious. Harvey, the legal AI platform backed by OpenAI, closed a $300 million Series D in February and announced expanded enterprise deployments across Am Law 100 firms including Latham & Watkins and A&O Shearman — relationships those firms have confirmed in press statements. The pitch to managing partners is straightforward: one Harvey seat handles document review, contract analysis, and first-draft memo work at a volume that previously required three to five junior associates or a small paralegal pool. The firms are not firing existing staff en masse; they are simply not backfilling. Non-backfilling is the invisible attrition. It...

Did a little Q1 review of where in the business AI is moving...

331 GenAI case studies were published in Q1 (up from 232 YoY)

Some clear patterns:

🟩 HR = the dark horse. AI recruiters are being used across the hiring process for efficiency. A notable example is high-volume hourly hiring for retail (e.g. chain restaurants, retail, and hospitality), using AI to text, screen and book interviews with candidates.
🟩 Sales orgs are going hard on AI, using it to rank the pipeline, score deals, forecast churn, and also using voice agents to qualify leads.
🟩 Legal departments and law firms of every size are now drafting motions, summarizing depositions, and reviewing contracts. Tools like Harvey are blowing up.
🟩 The growth in operations has a lot to do with a specific industry: Healthcare. Every doctor is using AI scribes now (AI that listens to patient visits and drafts the notes).
🟥 Knowledge management: the classic use case of "Enterprise search" aka building one big knowledge base to talk to your docs is plateauing in favour of more specific use cases, like support assistants for employees and synthesising research.
🟥 Customer service is moving away from simple Q&A chatbots (which are very widespread) to more agentic AI that can actually resolve customer requests end-to-end, rather than just be annoying.

The growth story of sales is the clearest: it sits close to the money, so it's an easy way for companies to show ROI on AI implementations — which in turn makes it easier to gain support for other AI initiatives.

HR, though: we all know what's REALLY happening here. Companies need AI recruiters to read 97,465 AI-written applications per day.

And now comes the shameless plug:

Most companies pick & prioritise AI use cases by brainstorming internally + guesswork. And up to 95% of initiatives fail.

What if you could validate your ideas against 2,000+ real implementations with quantified results?

You'll find use case intelligence at contextwindows dot ai

"And then HR said… We’ll get back to you."

For many 2025 MBA graduates, that line is not just a joke anymore — it’s the reality.
The job market has changed.
Companies are hiring — but more selectively.
General MBA profiles are struggling.
Callbacks are slower.
And fresh graduates are stuck refreshing inboxes.

Recent placement insights show:
• Hiring is becoming skill-specific, not degree-specific
• Many non-tech and general MBA roles saw ~15% drop in placements
• Recruiters now prefer analytics, AI, and domain-specialized profiles
• Even top B-schools reported students waiting longer for offers (LinkedIn)

This is not about lack of talent.
It’s about market expectations changing faster than curriculum.

What can 2025 MBA grads do?
→ Add domain skills (Analytics / HR Tech / Finance tools)
→ Build project-based portfolios
→ Network instead of only applying
→ Stay open to internships / contract roles
→ Keep learning while waiting

An MBA is still valuable — but callbacks now go to skills, not just degrees.
If you're a 2025 MBA graduate still waiting…
You're not alone.

Drop a "Still Waiting" in comments — let's connect and help each other.

Landing in a new market like Malaysia is an adrenaline rush for any MNC. You’ve got the strategy, the budget, and the vision. But then comes the "Real Estate Reality Check."

Traditionally, the move looks like this: sign a 3-year lease, spend 4-5 months navigating contractors, and sink a small fortune into a renovation that you can’t take with you if you scale faster than expected.

In 2026, that’s not just old school—it’s a massive operational risk. Here is why high-growth companies are ditching the "Hammer and Nails" approach for Flexible Private Office Suites.

1. Speed to Market is the New Currency
While your competitors are still arguing with interior designers over floor plans in KL Sentral, you could be fully operational. Flexible spaces allow your team to walk in, plug in, and start hiring on Day 1. No waiting for permits, no WiFi installation delays, and definitely no "renovation dust" on your first board meeting table.

2. The CAPEX vs. OPEX Game
Let’s talk numbers in the context of the Malaysian market today.
For a 2,000 sq. ft. Grade A office in a prime area like TR, KLCC or a formal office tower in Penang, a traditional lease will demand an upfront renovation cost of anywhere between RM 200,000 to RM 500,000 just to make it livable (think RM 100–RM 250 per sq. ft.). That’s capital "frozen" in the walls of a building you don’t own.

Conversely, a flexible private suite requires Zero CAPEX. You pay a higher monthly operational fee, but you keep your half-million Ringgit in the bank to fund your actual business—marketing, talent, and R&D.

3. Avoiding the "Reinstatement Trap"
In Malaysia, most commercial contracts have a "Reinstatement Clause." This means when your lease ends, you have to pay again to tear down those beautiful glass walls and return the space to a "bare shell." It’s effectively paying to build it and paying to destroy it. With a flexible co-working suite, you simply hand back the keys and walk away.

The Real Risks of Traditional Leasing for New Entries:

⛔ The Sunk Cost Risk: If you need to pivot or leave the state within 18 months, that RM 300k renovation is a 100% loss. You can’t liquidate a custom-built reception desk.

⛔ The Focus Risk: Your Country Manager should be focused on market share, not chasing plumbing contractors or managing electricity deposits with TNB.

⛔ The Scalability Risk: Traditional leases are rigid. If you grow from 20 to 50 employees in year one, you’re either stuck in a cramped office or paying massive penalties to break a lease.

For an MNC entering Malaysia, flexibility isn't just about "cool furniture." It’s a strategic hedge against uncertainty. Build your team, build your brand, and let the co-working provider worry about the lightbulbs and the lease terms.

Get "flexible" contract to land your office in Penang with low cost and quick move, look for Easyspace. www.easyspace.asia

Responsible AI starts with understanding what’s inside the model and that requires both legal and data science at the table

A Cambridge Law paper puts it clearly: AI systems learn on their own, and even their developers don’t always understand the internal logic behind their decisions.

That’s the black box problem.
And the challenge isn’t just technical, it’s a governance challenge.

When a legal team reviews an AI use case, they typically focus on what’s visible: the output, the vendor, the contract terms. 

What’s harder to assess from a purely legal lens is whether the model is learning from biased data, whether it’s amplifying historical patterns, or whether its predictions are confusing correlation with causation.

The paper cites a well-known case: Amazon built an AI tool to screen job applicants. It had to be scrapped after engineers discovered it was systematically downgrading CVs from women — because the model had been trained on historical hiring data dominated by men. The algorithm didn’t discriminate explicitly. It just learned from biased data. Nobody noticed until the damage was done.

That’s not a legal problem. That’s a data problem.

That’s why in Responsible AI governance, the question “does this comply with the regulation?” isn’t enough on its own. 

The question that changes the analysis is: has someone who understands the model validated how it reaches its conclusions?

Legal and Data Science perspectives aren’t competing in AI governance. They’re complementary. Without both at the table, risk assessment stays incomplete.

In a Porter’s Five Forces context, the threat of substitutes for Indian law firms is rising and structurally significant, especially due to technology, alternative service models, and regulatory shifts.

🔁 Threat of Substitutes for Indian Law Firms
1. Legal Tech & AI (High and Growing)
·        Tools for contract drafting, legal research, compliance, and document automation are replacing routine legal work. 
·        Platforms offering DIY legal solutions (templates, automated filings) reduce dependency on lawyers. 
·        AI-driven tools can perform tasks like due diligence, discovery, and research faster and cheaper. 
👉 Impact:
·        Reduces demand for low-value, repetitive legal services 
·        Pushes law firms toward high-value advisory roles 
📌 Evidence: Technology and automated platforms are increasingly substituting traditional services at lower cost 


2. Alternative Legal Service Providers (ALSPs) / LPOs
·        Legal Process Outsourcing (LPO) firms in India offer: 
Contract management 
Document review 
Compliance support 
·        These are cheaper, scalable, and process-driven 
👉 Impact:
·        Corporate clients shift back-office legal work away from law firms 
·        Law firms lose volume-based revenue streams 
📌 Evidence: Growth of LPOs and outsourcing models is a major substitute force 


3. In-house Legal Teams (Strong Substitute)
·        Large corporations are: 
Building robust internal legal departments 
Handling routine advisory, compliance, and contracts internally 
👉 Impact:
·        Law firms are used only for: 
Complex litigation 
Strategic transactions 
·        Reduces repeat billing opportunities 


4. Online Legal Platforms & Marketplaces
·        Platforms offering: 
Fixed-fee legal services 
On-demand lawyer access 
Startup-focused legal packages 
👉 Impact:
·        Price transparency + affordability attracts SMEs/startups 
·        Undercuts traditional hourly billing models 


5. Foreign Law Firms (Emerging Substitute/Hybrid Threat)
·        With gradual liberalization in India: 
Foreign firms can advise on international law, M&A, arbitration 
👉 Impact:
·        High-end corporate clients may prefer: 
Global expertise 
Integrated cross-border services 
📌 Evidence: Entry of foreign firms may reduce referrals and client share for Indian firms 


6. Self-Service & Standardization
·        Increasing use of: 
Templates 
Standard contracts 
Compliance checklists 
👉 Impact:
·        Individuals and startups avoid hiring lawyers for basic needs

I recently joined an insightful cross-industry dialogue in GLOBAL TECH AI ROUNDTABLE.
 It was an insightful discussion with leaders across BFSI, hardware, healthcare, food tech, and IT services, exploring how AI is evolving in each domain and the challenges ahead. 
Hardware leaders emphasized data–compute colocation to reduce GPU costs, while also pointing to latency, memory bandwidth, and token management issues. BFSI highlighted the human–AI synergy with defined roles and responsibilities, alongside regulatory hurdles. Healthcare stressed the need for unified orchestration across various departments like  diagnostics, billing, care divisions etc. Food tech impressed with fast‑track AI adoption through no‑code platforms that empower non‑tech teams to adopt AI. IT services revealed how AI reduces coordination overhead but raises complexities in contract renewals and client expectations.
 
Beyond the industry-specific hurdles, several universal truths emerged that every leader should consider:
·  Clear Shift: AI is moving from technical to business discussions, with 
         mindset gaps spanning decision‑makers, employees, and regulators etc.

·  Approach to AI Solutions: Many still apply deterministic software thinking, 
         but real value emerges when embracing non‑deterministic approaches.

·  Skill Gap: A clear gap exists in AI architecture and system thinking.

·  Cloud vs. On‑prem: Organizations prefer cloud for lightweight solutions, 
         but scale and cost challenges will  drive organization towards 
         hybrid/on‑prem adoption.

·  Team Structure: AI product lifecycles demand new team structures and  
        accountability models (intent lead, context engineer, prompt engineer, 
        system thinker, developer, data owner etc.).

·  AI Explainability: The real challenge lies in trust and workflow design — 
         ensuring solutions are explainable and reliable.

·  Shift in Hiring: Hiring is evolving to prioritize analytical thinkers and system 
        thinkers who can decompose problems and design value‑driven AI 
        solutions.

·  Selling AI Solutions: Selling is complex — beyond outcome‑based pricing, 
         clients demand deterministic cost models, benchmarking, and reliability.

My special thanks to Sohail Khan Ravikanth Chaganti Anupriya Singh Arpit Tandon Sandip Ghosh Hitesh B Jirawla Sandeep Momale Sunjeet Gupta for conducting such excellent session.
#AIadoption #AItransformation #AgenticAI

I spent 90 days tracking millions of dollars flowing into US, UK and East Asian startups. 

Why? To find where the high-paying remote  jobs appear before they hit job boards.
 
Here’s what I found:
Most lawyers look at job boards.
I looked at funding rounds.
And the pattern was so clear that it changed how I advise every legal professional who wants to work globally.

The 3‑step framework I now use to find legal opportunities before they’re even posted:
1. Follow the “Funding Signal.” 
When a company raises a Series A or B round, they aren’t just “hiring.” 

They are “Aggressively Scaling”

This explosion of capital triggers a tidal wave of complexity.

They need Legal Ops and Compliance in real time to safeguard new investments.

You want to be in their inbox before the job description exists. 

Because by the time the job hits LinkedIn, the first candidate is already in the room.

2. Audit your “Legal Tech Stack” 
Westlaw and LexisNexis are the baseline.
In the global remote market, AI prompt engineering is the new differentiator.

If you can show a CEO how you cut contract review time by 40% using AI, you stop being an expense, you become an asset they fight to keep.

3. Solve “Time‑Zone Anxiety” before they ask.
The biggest fear for a US/UK founder hiring in Africa or Asia isn’t your skill.
It’s the clock.
Don’t wait for them to bring it up.
Lead with: “I am 100% available for EST/GMT work hours – no exceptions.”
Remove the friction before they even feel it. That’s how you win.

Here’s the truth most people need to hear:
The bar isn’t higher for international roles.
The strategy is just different.

You don’t need a new degree.
You need a new roadmap.

Most companies think their biggest legal risk sits in their contracts. 

It doesn’t. 

It sits in their recruitment process. 

The CNIL has announced its 2026 enforcement priorities. 

One focus stands out: 
Automated decision-making in recruitment. 
This is not marginal. 

Many organisations now rely on: 
• CV screening tools 
• candidate scoring systems 
• algorithmic filtering 
Often without a clear audit trail. 

The risk is not only GDPR non-compliance. 

It is decision opacity. 

If a rejected candidate challenges the process, 
the question will be simple: 
Can you explain — and justify — the decision? 

For executive teams, this creates a structural exposure: 

• litigation risk 
• reputational risk 
• hiring bias risk 
• regulatory exposure under GDPR and AI Act 

In practice, very few companies can currently: 

• map where automated decisions actually occur 
• document the logic behind them 
• demonstrate meaningful human oversight 

The real question is not: 

“Are we using AI in recruitment?” 

But: 

“Would we be able to defend how it makes decisions?”

Staff augmentation just quietly became 5.2x bigger than managed services.

Here's what that means for 2026 CTOs.

The shift didn't happen overnight, but it did happen fast. Three years ago, most engineering leaders defaulted to "hire an agency" when they needed outside help. Today, they default to "embed a team."

The reason: control.

With a managed services engagement, you hand over a problem. The provider solves it, invoices you, and leaves. You don't own the code. You don't own the process. You don't own the knowledge.

With staff aug, the engineers work inside your systems. Your codebase. Your tools. Your Slack. Your process. When the engagement ends, everything stays inside your org.

Here's what this shift means practically for CTOs in 2026:

1/ Your AI strategy changes
You can't hand AI tooling over to an agency and expect them to get it right for your context. Staff aug gives you engineers who learn your AI stack and become part of how your team uses it. Agencies give you engineers who use their AI stack and leave when the contract ends.

2/ Your roadmap compresses
Staff aug teams don't need 60 days to understand your architecture, they embed and ship. A well-matched team is productive by week 2. An agency is productive by month 2, maybe month 3.

3/ Your talent pool globalizes
The best specialists for your problem probably aren't in your city. With staff aug, that stops mattering. A senior iOS engineer in Warsaw, a DevOps lead in São Paulo, an ML engineer in Bangalore, all working inside your repos, your sprints, your standups.

4/ Your fixed costs shrink
You scale teams up and down with your roadmap, not with your hiring plan. Product launch in Q2 needs +3 engineers for 4 months? Staff aug. Back to steady state in Q3? Release the augmentation. Your cap table stays clean. Your burn stays predictable.

5/ Your retention problem shifts
FTE retention is a permanent battle. Staff aug is time-boxed. You plan for the knowledge transfer from day one because the engagement has a known end date. You never get blindsided by a sudden resignation.

The companies winning this shift aren't treating staff aug as outsourcing. They're treating it as an extension of their team, with clear scope, clear ownership, and clear handoff.

The CTOs who get this in 2026 are going to ship 2-3x what the ones still defaulting to "hire an agency" ship.

What's your current split between FTE, staff aug, and managed services?

You cannot audit what you cannot see. And most organisations may not care beyond the chat screen.

AI in Audit: Separating facts from fiction. Part 2

Two publications I encourage IA practitioners should read on Auditing AI: COSO's "Achieving Effective Internal Control Over Generative AI" (February 2026) and ISO/IEC 42001, the world's first certifiable AI management system standard (December 2023). Together they define the floor for what AI assurance actually requires. 

The rest of this post draws from both.

1. It starts with an inventory.
Before you can audit AI, you need to know where it is. What tools are deployed. What processes they touch. Who owns them. What decisions they influence. 
In many organisations, that inventory does not exist. Shadow AI, tools adopted informally without IT or risk sign-off, is not a fringe problem. 

2. Then it becomes a controls problem.
AI governance is not a technology problem. It is an internal control problem. It must be designed, operated, and evidenced. 

What Internal Audit should actually be looking at:
- Data quality and lineage. AI outputs are only as reliable as the data fed into them. Every downstream process inherits whatever is compromised at the input stage.
- Human oversight mechanisms. Who, if at all, reviews AI outputs before they influence a decision? Is that review documented and evidenced? Many organisations cannot answer either question.
- Change management as models are updated. AI models drift. An output accurate in January may not be in June. Recalibration schedules and monitoring logic need their own controls.
- Third-party and vendor AI risk. Most organisations are not building AI. They are buying it. The risk sits partly outside direct control, constrained by contract terms and vendor cooperation.
- Bias and fairness testing. Critical wherever AI influences decisions affecting real people. Credit. Hiring. Claims. Complaints. Not an optional add-on.
- Explainability of outputs. Can the organisation explain why the AI reached a decision? Regulators increasingly require it. Most organisations cannot answer it.
- Regulatory alignment. EU AI Act. Data localisation. Sector-specific rules. The regulatory landscape is moving faster than most policies can cope.

3. And here is the honest part.
A joint IIA Foundation and AuditBoard report from February 2026, found that fewer than 40% of IA functions believe they are adequately prepared to detect or respond to AI-enabled risks. Broader AI assurance readiness is lower still, with only 28% of IA leaders confident in their ability to audit AI risks effectively.

That is not a readiness gap. That is an assurance gap the Audit Committee should be asking about directly. Particularly when management's AI rollout is outpacing every control function in the building.

Part 3 will cover the other side. Auditing with AI. Practical use cases in the audit lifecycle. 

Stay tuned.

#InternalAudit #AuditCommittee #ArtificialIntelligence #AIGovernance #IIA

"Indian enterprises won't pay."

We hear this constantly. And at first glance, the numbers support the frustration:

→ Average enterprise sales cycle in India: 9 to 14 months for new AI vendor relationships (per Redseer Strategy Consultants's 2025 SaaS study)
→ Unpaid PoC requests: cited by over 70% of Indian B2B founders as a top friction point
→ Payment delays of 60 to 120 days post-contract are standard in many large enterprise segments

These are real numbers. The friction is real.

But here's what the same data shows about the 30% of Indian AI startups that are winning enterprise deals:

They have, on average, one key differentiator in their commercial team, someone who has previously worked inside the type of enterprise they are now selling to.

Not someone who sold to enterprises. Someone who was inside one. Who knows where the bodies are buried in the procurement process. Who has a personal relationship with the kind of CISO or CDO they need to reach.

This is the insider-to-outsider hire. It's the most underused playbook in Indian deeptech sales.

Instead of hiring a SaaS sales leader who has always been on the vendor side, you hire the ex-Head of Digital Transformation at a Tata group company, someone who used to be the buyer, who can now guide your product and your pitch to fit the buyer's actual decision-making reality.

That profile requires patient, specific search. It doesn't show up on a job post.

When we work with AI founders who are stuck in the 'Skip India' loop, the first question we ask is: who on your team has ever sat on the other side of the table from you?

Usually, the answer is nobody.

That's where we start.

#EnterpriseSales #InsiderHire #AIIndia #TalentIntelligence #QuellSearch

🚀 IT Staffing Trends: What’s Shaping Tech Hiring in 2026

Tech hiring in 2026 is no longer about filling seats—it’s about finding the right skills, faster.

The market is shifting from volume hiring to strategic, skill-based hiring, with demand rising for AI, cloud, cybersecurity, and data talent. Companies are becoming more selective and prioritizing roles tied to growth and transformation.

What’s driving the change?

✔ AI is reshaping teams – companies want high-impact talent that can work with automation tools
✔ Skills over degrees – practical expertise matters more than traditional credentials
✔ Flexible workforce models – contract, hybrid, and augmented teams are growing
✔ Faster hiring cycles – long recruitment timelines are losing relevance
✔ Global talent access – businesses are hiring beyond geography

💡 The companies winning in 2026 won’t be the ones hiring the most people—they’ll be the ones hiring the smartest.

If your hiring strategy still looks like 2024, it may already be outdated.

Role Overview The MLOps is responsible for building and maintaining the full machine learning lifecycle across cloud and on-prem environments. This includes integrating data pipelines, labeling flows, training pipelines, testing frameworks, model optimization, 
 
Key Responsibilities End-to-End MLOps Pipeline Development • Build and maintain automated end-to-end ML pipelines covering data ingestion, dataset management, labeling workflows, training, validation, optimization, deployment, and monitoring. • Ensure ML pipelines run in both cloud and on-prem GPU clusters, with strong reproducibility and traceability. Data Labeling Integration • Integrate data pipelines with labeling platforms and automate dataset creation and quality checks. • Work closely with the Label Manager to enforce labeling quality gates and track dataset KPIs. Training, Testing Optimization • Support AI Engineers by automating training workflows, hyperparameter tuning, experiment tracking, and benchmarking. • Integrate optimization steps performance degradation, anomalies, and operational metrics. • Build dashboards and alerts to monitor model performance across simulation and on-vehicle tests. Collaboration Cross-Functional Alignment • Work closely with: o AI Engineers on training and evaluation o Framework Engineers on model optimization o Application Engineers integrating ML outputs into the autonomy stack o Data Engineering on scalable data and storage architecture o ML Architect on integration and implementation of new components • Drive best practices for MLOps, documentation, and standardized ML workflows across the department. • Steer and guide MLOPS engineers. ________________________________________
 
Required Skills Experience • Bachelor’s or Master’s degree in Engineering, Computer Science, Robotics, or related field. • 5+ years(Tech lead) of experience in data labeling, data operations, data quality, or ML dataset management—preferably in autonomous driving. • Strong programming skills in Python, with familiarity in C++ preferred. • Hands-on experience with MLOps frameworks: MLflow, Kubeflow, Airflow, Argo, Azure ML, Databricks or similar. • Strong understanding of Docker, Kubernetes, CI/CD, and distributed training. • Experience with data pipeline technologies (Kafka, Spark, Delta Lake, Databricks). • Good understanding of multimodal autonomous driving data (camera, LiDAR, radar).

Jamie Tso and Raymond Sun describe a community building a shared second brain. The people Jamie describes are also candidates. And the tools they have built for themselves do not stay at home during interviews.

A candidate today does not ask whether the company has Zoom or Teams. The answer is taken for granted. 

Now replace Zoom with AI. Most candidates do not yet ask "what is your AI stack?" during interviews, though that is starting to change. 

It is already a routine question, and I have seen conversations where AI-proficient lawyers treat the answer as a clear deal breaker.

Some people worth hiring are turning up with tools they have built themselves, whether AI agents, skill libraries, or retrieval systems trained over evenings and weekends.

Take LegalQuants as an example. Alongside a series of open-sourced prototypes across governance, arbitration, and compliance workflows, they have built a proprietary second brain. It combines a structured knowledge base with a library of /skills that perform specific legal tasks on demand, such as drafting support, document review, or procedural management. Some of those tools run deterministically and do not require a call to an LLM at all, which changes the compliance conversation completely.

After a security and compliance review, tools of that kind could run on company infrastructure without routing a single byte of company data outside it.

Which raises a question that, inside most companies, nobody currently owns: who handles the onboarding of those tools?

Security review knows how to vet software, IP counsel knows how to handle pre-existing works, and knowledge management knows how to share assets across teams. None currently owns the employee who shows up on day one with a working agent and a personal skills library, so the tool ends up sitting unused, getting adopted informally, or being quietly absorbed into the company stack with nobody quite clear on what terms.

The IP question is the difficult one. When something is built on personal time and then refined during work hours against real company problems, does the employee leave with it when they go, or does the company acquire a perpetual licence? Could new hires negotiate a carve-out for pre-existing tooling, the way consulting engagements have IP schedules? And even when the contracts are clear, would employees actually want to share tools they consider part of their professional identity, the way a chef does not hand over his secret sauce?

A company that funds a token allowance and runs a proper onboarding track for employee-built tools will soon have a joining argument, while a company that insists on vendor-only tooling may have a retention problem.

The function that handles all this does not yet have a name. It sits somewhere between IT, Legal, and HR. How soon before it needs to be its own thing?

Based on current Australian hiring trends, these are the legal practice areas companies are hiring for most right now:

Corporate / Commercial (Generalist)
 -Employers want commercially minded lawyers who can handle contracts, governance, projects and day‑to‑day business risk.

Construction/Infrastructure/Projects
 -Always a strong demand for experienced lawyers with solid front-end construction/projects experience

Privacy, Data & Technology Law
 -The fastest‑growing area. Privacy Act reform, data governance, cyber risk and AI regulation are driving strong demand across banks, telcos, tech, retail and healthcare.

Regulatory & Compliance
 -Particularly strong in financial services, funds, energy and healthcare. 

Employment & Workplace Relations
 -Consistently needed across Australian employers

We are hiring for a Senior Data Scientist
Experience- 8+years
Location-Remote
Mode-Contract
Share your cv - sanjana.patel@huemot.com

Job Summary
We are looking for a highly skilled Senior Data Scientist with 7+ years of experience to join our remote team. The ideal candidate will have deep expertise in statistical analysis, machine learning, and data engineering, along with the ability to translate complex data into actionable business insights. You will work cross-functionally with product, engineering, and business stakeholders to design and deploy scalable data-driven solutions.
Key Responsibilities
Design, develop, and deploy advanced machine learning models and predictive analytics solutions.
Analyze large, complex datasets to uncover trends, patterns, and actionable insights.
Build and optimize data pipelines and workflows for scalable analytics.
Collaborate with cross-functional teams to define business problems and translate them into data science solutions.
Communicate findings and recommendations to stakeholders through data visualization and storytelling.
Implement and maintain end-to-end ML lifecycle (model development, validation, deployment, monitoring).
Mentor junior data scientists and contribute to best practices.
Stay updated with the latest advancements in AI/ML and data science technologies.
Required Skills & Qualifications
7+ years of experience in Data Science, Machine Learning, or related roles.
Strong proficiency in Python (NumPy, Pandas, Scikit-learn, TensorFlow/PyTorch).
Expertise in statistical modeling, hypothesis testing, and predictive analytics.
Experience with big data technologies (Spark, Hadoop).
Hands-on experience with cloud platforms (AWS, Azure, or GCP).
Strong knowledge of SQL and database systems.
Experience with data visualization tools (Tableau, Power BI, Matplotlib, Seaborn).
Solid understanding of MLOps practices and CI/CD pipelines.
Excellent problem-solving and communication skills.
Preferred Qualifications
Master’s or PhD in Data Science, Computer Science, Statistics, or related field.
Experience in deploying models using Docker, Kubernetes.
Familiarity with NLP, Deep Learning, or Computer Vision.
Experience working in Agile/Scrum environments.
#Python #DataScientist #MLOPS #AWS #Tableau
